name: Harm Aversion & Moral Decision-Making (Crockett)
description: The canonical computational study of moral decision-making: participants choose between options trading off money against painful electric shocks delivered either to themselves or to an anonymous stranger. A computational model estimates each person's 'harm aversion' — the subjective exchange rate between money and pain — separately for self and other, revealing that most people are 'hyper-altruistic', requiring more compensation to harm others than themselves. The 2017 fMRI study links these model-derived values to activity in the brain's valuation network. Raw data are available from the authors on request; the paradigm and computational model are fully specified in the papers, so the behavioral analysis can be reproduced from simulation.
citation: Crockett MJ, Siegel JZ, Kurth-Nelson Z, Dayan P, Dolan RJ (2017). Moral transgressions corrupt neural representations of value. Nature Neuroscience, 20(6), 879-885.

publications.title: Harm to others outweighs harm to self in moral decision making (PNAS)
